landing.title:
Learning Landscape
Sustainable Digitalization
landing.tagline: Travel through four worlds and discover how you can work more sustainably as an IT professional.

avatar.subtitle: Enter your name and choose your companion for the expedition.

mobile.jungle.desc.geraete-lichtung: How long does your laptop live – and why does it matter?
mobile.jungle.desc.cloud-quelle: What really happens when you upload data to the cloud?
mobile.jungle.desc.code-camp: Small settings, big impact – saving energy while coding.
mobile.ocean.desc.server-riff: Data centers consume more electricity than some countries.
mobile.ocean.desc.streaming-strom: What does a video really cost – in energy, not money?
mobile.ocean.desc.backup-bucht: Do you store smartly – or just everything?
mobile.cosmos.desc.ide-asteroid: Your IDE uses more power than you think – optimize it!
mobile.cosmos.desc.deploy-stern: Every build uses energy – how do you deploy efficiently?
mobile.metro.desc.ki-kraftwerk: Artificial intelligence needs enormous amounts of energy – how do you deal with it?
mobile.metro.desc.open-source-platz: Open source as a sustainable alternative – sharing instead of developing twice.
mobile.metro.desc.digital-ethics-turm: Responsibility in the digital world – who decides what is sustainable?
mobile.finale.title: The Finale Celebration
station.geraete-lichtung.title: The Device Clearing
station.geraete-lichtung.intro: You enter a sunny clearing. Before you lies an old laptop, half overgrown with moss. It still works – but for how long? And what's really inside it?
station.geraete-lichtung.micro.p1: Have you ever wondered what's inside your laptop? A single smartphone contains over 60 different chemical elements – many of them rare earths, mined under problematic conditions.
station.geraete-lichtung.micro.keyfact1: Manufacturing a single laptop produces approx. 300–400 kg CO₂. That's more than the device's total electricity consumption during its entire lifespan.
station.geraete-lichtung.micro.p2: This means: The biggest ecological lever is not saving power during use, but extending the device's lifespan.
station.geraete-lichtung.micro.list:
• Extending lifespan from 3 to 5 years = approx. 30% less ecological footprint
  • Replace battery or upgrade RAM instead of buying a new device
  • SSD upgrade on older devices often gives a significant performance boost
  • Cleaning fans and renewing thermal paste keeps devices cool and alive
station.geraete-lichtung.micro.keyfact2: Switzerland produces approx. 23 kg of e-waste per capita per year – one of the highest rates worldwide.
station.geraete-lichtung.action.p1: Find out how old your device is – and think of a concrete measure to extend its lifespan.
station.geraete-lichtung.action.instruct:
• macOS: Apple Menu → About This Mac → Overview
  • Windows: Win + R → type msinfo32 → System Summary
  • Linux: Terminal → sudo dmidecode -t system
station.geraete-lichtung.action.list2:
• How old is your device?
  • What concrete measure could extend its lifespan?
station.geraete-lichtung.reflection.q: What factors influence your decision to buy a new device – and how many of them are truly technically necessary?
station.geraete-lichtung.reflection.hint: Often it's software updates, trends, or the desire for new features – not the hardware itself – that make a device seem "old". A device that still works is not an old device.
station.geraete-lichtung.challenge.p1: Research: Is there a way to upgrade your oldest device at home or give it a new purpose?
station.geraete-lichtung.challenge.p2: Ideas: Home server, media center, learning PC for younger siblings, Linux installation for new life.
station.geraete-lichtung.challenge.place: Which device did you choose and what is your idea? (2–3 sentences)
station.cloud-quelle.title: The Cloud Spring
station.cloud-quelle.intro: Deep in the jungle you find a shimmering spring. Data flows like water – invisible, but not without traces.
station.cloud-quelle.micro.p1: "The Cloud" sounds light and invisible. In reality, it consists of massive data centers that consume enormous amounts of electricity and water (for cooling).
station.cloud-quelle.micro.keyfact1: Worldwide, data centers consume approx. 1–1.5% of global electricity – with a strong upward trend.
station.cloud-quelle.micro.p2: Every file you save, every video you stream, every backup that syncs has an energy cost:
station.cloud-quelle.micro.list:
• 1 hour of HD streaming consumes approx. 1.5–3 kWh of infrastructure energy
  • An email with a 5 MB attachment causes around 50g CO₂ (incl. server storage and redundancy)
  • Cloud providers typically store your data 3x redundantly at different locations
station.cloud-quelle.micro.keyfact2: Your 10 GB in the cloud is actually 30 GB on servers.
station.cloud-quelle.micro.p3: In Switzerland, many companies use hyperscalers like AWS, Azure, or GCP. Some of these providers operate data centers in and around Zurich.
station.cloud-quelle.action.p1: Clean up your cloud storage and review your synchronizations.
station.cloud-quelle.action.task1:
• Open your cloud storage (OneDrive, Google Drive, iCloud, or Dropbox)
  • Sort by file size
  • Delete or archive at least 3 files you no longer need
station.cloud-quelle.action.task2:
• Review your automatic backups and synchronizations
  • Which folders are being synchronized?
  • Do you really need everything in the cloud?
station.cloud-quelle.reflection.q: If cloud storage were free and unlimited – would you change your behavior? What does that say about the connection between cost and sustainability?
station.cloud-quelle.reflection.hint: Free doesn't mean resource-free. The costs are just paid elsewhere – through energy, raw materials, and CO₂. When something is "free", the signal that motivates us to use it consciously is often missing.
station.cloud-quelle.challenge.p1: Find out in which region your cloud provider stores your data. Does this data center use renewable energy?
station.cloud-quelle.challenge.p2: Tip: Search for the "Sustainability Report" of your provider (e.g. "Microsoft sustainability report" or "Google environmental report").
station.cloud-quelle.challenge.placehold: Which cloud provider do you use and what did you find out about its sustainability?
station.code-camp.title: The Code Camp
station.code-camp.intro: At the campfire of the Code Camp you meet other developers. Tonight's topic: How much energy does your setup actually consume?
station.code-camp.micro.p1: Modern IDEs like VS Code, IntelliJ, or Visual Studio are powerful – but they also consume significant CPU and RAM, especially with many extensions.
station.code-camp.micro.p2: Every active extension runs as a background process: Linters, formatters, language servers, AI copilots – all need resources.
station.code-camp.micro.keyfact1: VS Code with 30+ extensions can easily use 1–2 GB RAM – a "lean" setup with only the essentials saves 200–500 MB.
station.code-camp.micro.list1:
• Dark Themes on OLED displays save up to 60% energy compared to light themes
  • Auto-Save with very short intervals triggers constant disk writes and extension re-evaluations
  • Features like Real-Time Type Checking or AI Autocomplete are CPU-intensive – are they always worth it?
station.code-camp.micro.keyfact2: Not every tool that's "handy" needs to run all the time. Activating consciously instead of always-on is often the better way.
station.code-camp.action.p1: Optimize your IDE setup – work leaner, use fewer resources.
station.code-camp.action.task1:
• Open your IDE and count your installed extensions/plugins
  • VS Code: Ctrl+Shift+X (or Cmd+Shift+X) → "Installed"
  • Disable at least 2 extensions that you don't regularly need
  • Right-click → "Disable" (don't delete – you can always re-enable them)
station.code-camp.action.task2:
• Do you have an OLED display? Activate a dark theme.
  • Check your auto-save setting: Files: Auto Save to afterDelay with at least 1000ms instead of instant
station.code-camp.reflection.q: Where is the boundary between productivity and resource consumption? When is an extension worth the energy – and when not?
station.code-camp.reflection.hint: An extension that saves you 30 minutes per day is worth the power. One you need once a month can be activated on demand. The question is not "all or nothing" but "choose consciously".
station.code-camp.challenge.list:
• "Lean" – only the essentials (language, formatter, Git)
  • "Full" – everything you need for special projects
station.code-camp.challenge.p3: Switch to the Lean profile for a week and report whether you notice a difference.
station.code-camp.challenge.placeholder: Which extensions did you keep in the Lean profile? Did you notice a difference?
station.server-riff.title: The Server Reef
station.server-riff.intro: Deep in the ocean you encounter a glowing reef – but it's not made of coral, but of endless rows of blinking servers. Here pulses the heart of the cloud. But how much energy does this reef actually consume?
station.server-riff.micro.p1: Every time you upload a file to the cloud, train a model, or deploy a container, a data center is working for you somewhere. Worldwide, data centers consume approx. 1–2% of global electricity – and rising.
station.server-riff.micro.keyfact1: A single large data center needs as much electricity as a small city of 30,000 inhabitants. Up to 40% of the energy goes to cooling alone.
station.server-riff.micro.p2: To measure data center efficiency, there's the PUE value (Power Usage Effectiveness):
station.server-riff.micro.list1:
• PUE 1.0 = perfect – every kilowatt-hour goes to computing
  • PUE 1.2 = very good (Google's average)
  • PUE 1.6 = industry average – 60% overhead for cooling, lighting, etc.
  • PUE 2.0 = poor – half the energy is "waste"
station.server-riff.micro.keyfact2: That's why many data centers are located in Norway, Ireland, or Sweden: The cool climate massively reduces cooling needs. Google even uses seawater for cooling in Finland.
station.server-riff.micro.p3: Switzerland also has smart approaches: Waste heat from data centers is fed into the district heating network. In Zurich, Green.ch's data center already heats apartments – server waste heat becomes heating energy.
station.server-riff.micro.list2:
• Microsoft tested an experimental underwater data center (Project Natick)
  • Meta uses 100% renewable energy at many locations
  • AWS offers a "Customer Carbon Footprint" view in the console
station.server-riff.action.p1: Find out where your cloud provider operates its data centers – and research the PUE value or sustainability strategy.
station.server-riff.action.instructions:
• Azure: Search for "Azure Sustainability" – Microsoft publishes location data and PUE values
  • AWS: In the AWS Console → Customer Carbon Footprint Tool under "Billing"
  • Google Cloud: Carbon Footprint Dashboard in the Cloud Console menu
  • Own server / hoster: Ask about the electricity certificate and PUE value
station.server-riff.action.list2:
• In which region do your cloud services run?
  • What PUE value does your provider have (if published)?
  • Does the provider use renewable energy?
station.server-riff.reflection.q: Should there be a label for sustainable cloud services – like for food?
station.server-riff.reflection.hint: With food, labels like "Organic" or "Fairtrade" help with purchasing decisions. For cloud services, nothing like that exists. Imagine you could see for every deployment: "This service runs in a data center with PUE 1.1, 100% hydropower, Region Norway." Would that influence your decisions? And who should issue such a label – the industry itself or an independent body?
station.server-riff.challenge.p1: Compare two cloud providers (e.g. Azure vs. AWS or Google Cloud vs. Infomaniak) regarding their sustainability.
station.server-riff.challenge.p2: Research the following points for both providers:
station.server-riff.challenge.list:
• Average PUE value
  • Share of renewable energy
  • Data center locations
  • Transparency tools for customers (CO₂ dashboard etc.)
station.server-riff.challenge.p3: Record your result in a short comparison table.
station.server-riff.challenge.placeholde: Which two providers did you compare? What was your result (PUE, energy, location)?
station.streaming-strom.title: The Streaming Current
station.streaming-strom.intro: You're caught in a massive current – billions of data packets racing past you. Videos, music, podcasts: an endless stream of digital content. But what does this convenience cost our planet?
station.streaming-strom.micro.p1: Streaming feels "light" – no physical media, no shipping. But behind every video is a whole chain of servers, networks, and devices consuming energy.
station.streaming-strom.micro.keyfact1: One hour of HD video streaming produces approx. 36 g CO₂. Sounds like little? At 2 hours daily, that's over 26 kg CO₂ per year – just from streaming.
station.streaming-strom.micro.list1:
• Netflix and YouTube together make up about 15% of total global internet traffic
  • A 4K stream uses approx. 7 GB per hour – a 720p stream only about 0.9 GB
  • That's a factor of 8 in data volume – often with barely visible difference on small screens
  • Audio streaming (Spotify, podcasts) uses approx. 50–150 MB per hour – a fraction of video
station.streaming-strom.micro.keyfact2: CDNs (Content Delivery Networks) like Cloudflare or Akamai cache content closer to users. This shortens transmission paths and saves energy. Without CDNs, the internet's energy consumption would be significantly higher.
station.streaming-strom.micro.p3: What many don't know: The network itself also uses electricity. Every router, every switch, every fiber-optic connection from the data center to your device needs energy. WiFi is more efficient than mobile data – 4G/5G uses up to 5x more energy than a WiFi connection.
station.streaming-strom.micro.list2:
• Leaving a music video running on YouTube when you're only listening to the song? Unnecessary.
  • Autoplay on social media = endless streams nobody consciously chose
  • Downloads instead of streaming saves energy for content you consume multiple times
station.streaming-strom.action.p1: Optimize your streaming settings – you save data volume and energy without really sacrificing quality.
station.streaming-strom.action.instructi:
• YouTube: Settings → Video Quality → Enable "Data Saver" or manually set to 720p
  • Netflix: Account → Profile → Playback Settings → Set data usage to "Medium"
  • Spotify: Settings → Audio Quality → "Normal" instead of "Very High" for mobile streaming
  • Social Media: Disable video autoplay (Instagram, TikTok, Twitter/X)
station.streaming-strom.action.list2:
• Use WiFi instead of mobile data when possible
  • Download playlists and podcasts in advance instead of streaming repeatedly
  • Listen to music as audio – not as a music video
station.streaming-strom.reflection.q: At what point does convenience become waste – and who defines that boundary?
station.streaming-strom.reflection.hint: Nobody says you should stop streaming. But the defaults are often set to maximum – 4K on a 13-inch screen, autoplay everywhere, background videos. The question is not "abstinence or not" but: Are you making a conscious choice? Platforms are designed for you to consume as much as possible. If you don't define the boundary yourself, you're letting the algorithm set it.
station.streaming-strom.challenge.p2: Estimate how many hours per week you use the following services, and calculate:
station.streaming-strom.challenge.list:
• Video streaming (HD): approx. 36 g CO₂ per hour
  • Video streaming (4K): approx. 55 g CO₂ per hour
  • Audio streaming: approx. 7 g CO₂ per hour
  • Video calls (camera on): approx. 30 g CO₂ per hour
station.streaming-strom.challenge.p3: Formula: Hours per week × 4.3 × CO₂ value = monthly footprint
station.streaming-strom.challenge.p4: Document your result and consider where you could most easily save 20%.
station.streaming-strom.challenge.placeh: What is your monthly streaming CO₂ footprint? Where can you save 20%?
station.backup-bucht.title: The Backup Bay
station.backup-bucht.intro: You reach a quiet bay. On the seabed lie thousands of sunken crates – data that nobody needs anymore, but that still takes up space and energy. How much digital ballast are you carrying?
station.backup-bucht.micro.p1: Storage has become cheap – and that's exactly the problem. We hoard data because there's no reason to delete it. But "free" doesn't mean "free for the environment".
station.backup-bucht.micro.keyfact1: Worldwide, approx. 64 zettabytes of data were created in 2020. Over 90% of this data is never read again. It just sits there – on servers running 24/7.
station.backup-bucht.micro.p2: This phenomenon has a name: Dark Data. Data that is collected, stored, and forgotten – without ever being analyzed or used. According to Gartner, it makes up over 50% of stored enterprise data.
station.backup-bucht.micro.list:
• Every stored GB uses electricity – whether on your local SSD or in the cloud
  • Cloud storage is replicated three times (for resilience) – 1 GB in the cloud = approx. 3 GB on servers
  • Old backups, forgotten repos, unused container images: all digital ballast
  • Email attachments you never open are stored on mail servers for years
station.backup-bucht.micro.keyfact2: The 3-2-1 backup rule helps you back up smartly instead of excessively: 3 copies of your data, on 2 different media types, with 1 at an external location. That's all you need in most cases.
station.backup-bucht.micro.p3: Sustainable data management doesn't mean "never store anything" but consciously deciding what to keep – and consistently deleting the rest.
station.backup-bucht.action.p1: Delete 1 GB of unnecessary data now – sounds like little, but the effect multiplies through cloud replication and backup cycles.
station.backup-bucht.action.instructions:
• Downloads folder: Open your Downloads folder and sort by date – anything older than 3 months is probably unnecessary
  • Cloud storage: Google Drive / OneDrive / iCloud → sort by size and check the largest files
  • Email: Search for emails with large attachments (> 5 MB) – often old presentations or ZIP files
  • Photos: Sort out duplicate images, screenshots, and failed shots
  • Git/Dev: Old node_modules, unused Docker images (docker image prune), orphaned branches
station.backup-bucht.action.list2:
• How much storage did you free up?
  • What surprised you the most?
  • How old were the oldest files you found?
station.backup-bucht.reflection.q: What if storage were more expensive – would you handle data differently?
station.backup-bucht.reflection.hint: Storage used to be scarce and expensive – you carefully considered what to save. Today storage is so cheap that we keep everything "just in case". But cheap for us doesn't mean cheap for the environment. Imagine every GB cost you 1 CHF per month: How quickly would you start cleaning up? Maybe we don't need a higher price – just the same care we naturally apply to physical things.
station.backup-bucht.challenge.p1: Create a personal "Data Cleanup Plan" for the next 30 days.
station.backup-bucht.challenge.p2: Plan a specific area for each week:
station.backup-bucht.challenge.list:
• Week 1: Clean up downloads folder and desktop
  • Week 2: Go through cloud storage (Drive, OneDrive, iCloud)
  • Week 3: Clean out email inbox (unsubscribe from old newsletters, delete large attachments)
  • Week 4: Clean up dev environment (old projects, containers, packages)
station.backup-bucht.challenge.p3: Set yourself a concrete goal (e.g. 5 GB in 30 days) and track your progress.
station.backup-bucht.challenge.placehold: What is your concrete goal (e.g. 5 GB in 30 days)? Describe your 4-week plan.
station.ide-asteroid.title: The IDE Asteroid
station.ide-asteroid.intro: On this asteroid, everything revolves around your development environment. How much energy does your IDE setup really consume – and what can you do about it?
station.ide-asteroid.micro.p1: VS Code, IntelliJ, WebStorm – modern IDEs are powerful tools. But they run on Electron (VS Code) or the JVM (JetBrains) and consume significant resources.
station.ide-asteroid.micro.keyfact1: VS Code with 30+ extensions easily uses 1–2 GB RAM and keeps your CPU busy with dozens of background processes – linters, language servers, AI copilots, formatters.
station.ide-asteroid.micro.p2: Every extension runs as its own process. That means:
station.ide-asteroid.micro.list1:
• More extensions = more RAM and CPU load
  • Idle processes keep running even when you don't need them
  • Electron-based apps (VS Code, Slack, Discord) are essentially full browsers – each needs its own resources
station.ide-asteroid.micro.keyfact2: Dark Mode on OLED displays saves up to 60% screen energy because black pixels are completely turned off. On LCD displays the effect is smaller, but your eyes will still thank you.
station.ide-asteroid.micro.p3: More energy hogs in your IDE:
station.ide-asteroid.micro.list2:
• Auto-Save with instant interval triggers disk writes and extension re-evaluations with every keystroke – 1000ms delay is enough
  • Telemetry & Crash Reporting sends data in the background – that uses network and CPU
  • Real-Time Type Checking and AI Autocomplete are CPU-intensive and not always necessary
  • Unclosed IDE windows remain in the Task Manager as zombie processes
station.ide-asteroid.micro.keyfact3: Tip: Open the Task Manager (Windows: Ctrl+Shift+Esc, macOS: Activity Monitor) and be amazed at how many processes your IDE starts.
station.ide-asteroid.action.p1: Make your IDE setup leaner – fewer resources, same productivity.
station.ide-asteroid.action.task1:
• Open VS Code and go to Extensions: Ctrl+Shift+X (or Cmd+Shift+X)
  • Filter by "Installed" and scroll through the list
  • Disable at least 3 extensions you don't regularly need
  • Right-click → "Disable" (don't delete – you can always re-enable them)
station.ide-asteroid.action.task2:
• Activate a dark theme: Ctrl+Shift+P → Color Theme → choose a dark theme
  • Disable telemetry: Settings → search telemetry.telemetryLevel → set to off
station.ide-asteroid.action.task3:
• Open Task Manager / Activity Monitor
  • Sort by Memory
  • Note how much RAM your IDE currently uses
  • Close zombie processes from no longer open projects
station.ide-asteroid.reflection.q: Do we really need an IDE that runs like a browser – or could it be lighter?
station.ide-asteroid.reflection.hint: Electron apps like VS Code are convenient because they work cross-platform. But for simple tasks, sometimes a lightweight editor like Sublime Text, Vim, or even Notepad++ is enough. The question is not "all or nothing" but: Does the tool fit the task? For a quick config file you don't need a full-stack IDE monster.
station.ide-asteroid.challenge.list:
• Open the Task Manager and take a screenshot of your IDE's RAM usage with all extensions
  • Then disable all non-essential extensions and restart the IDE
  • Take a second screenshot and compare
  • Share your comparison with your class or instructor
station.ide-asteroid.challenge.placehold: How much RAM did your IDE use before vs. after? Which extensions did you disable?
station.deploy-stern.title: The Deploy Star
station.deploy-stern.intro: The Deploy Star shines brighter with every build – but every CI/CD run costs energy. How can you make your pipelines more efficient?
station.deploy-stern.micro.p1: Every time you push and a CI/CD pipeline kicks in, a server starts somewhere, installs dependencies, builds your project, and runs tests. That uses real computing time on real servers – and thus electricity.
station.deploy-stern.micro.keyfact1: An average GitHub Actions run (build + test) takes 2–10 minutes. With 20 pushes per day in a team, that quickly adds up to 200 minutes of server time per day – for a single project.
station.deploy-stern.micro.list1:
• Unnecessary Rebuilds – every push triggers a full build, even when only a README changed
  • npm install on every run – thousands of packages re-downloaded instead of cached
  • Docker Images without layer caching – every layer rebuilt every time
  • Duplicate Pipelines – build runs on push AND on pull request, i.e. twice
station.deploy-stern.micro.keyfact2: Docker Layer Caching can reduce build times by 50–80%. And pnpm instead of npm saves up to 60% disk space and is often 2–3x faster at installing.
station.deploy-stern.micro.list2:
• Caching – dependencies, Docker layers, build artifacts
  • Conditional Builds – only build when relevant files changed (paths filter in GitHub Actions)
  • Monorepo tools like Nx or Turborepo only build what changed
  • Concurrency cancellation – when a new push comes, stop old runs
  • Lightweight Runner – not every job needs a beefy VM
station.deploy-stern.micro.keyfact3: GitHub gives every free account 2000 Actions minutes/month. If you're hitting that, it's a sign your pipelines are running wastefully.
station.deploy-stern.action.p1: Analyze your own pipeline – how efficient is your build process really?
station.deploy-stern.action.task1:
• Open a project on GitHub and go to Actions
  • Count: How many runs happened in the last week?
  • How long does an average run take?
  • Are there runs that were unnecessary (e.g. documentation-only changes)?
station.deploy-stern.action.task2:
• Open your .github/workflows/*.yml file
  • Check if actions/cache is used. If not, add it
  • Use actions/setup-node@v4 with cache: 'npm' for automatic Node caching
  • Alternative: Manual caching with actions/cache@v4 on node_modules
station.deploy-stern.action.task3:
• Add concurrency to your workflow so old runs are cancelled when a new push comes
  • Set cancel-in-progress: true to stop running jobs on new push
  • This prevents duplicate builds on the same branch
station.deploy-stern.reflection.q: Is it better to deploy fast and often – or less frequently and more consciously?
station.deploy-stern.reflection.hint: DevOps culture says: "Ship early, ship often." But that doesn't mean every commit needs to go straight to the pipeline. Batching changes, conscious push timing, and good branch strategies can drastically reduce the number of runs – without sacrificing quality. It's about the sweet spot between agility and resource consciousness.
station.deploy-stern.challenge.list1:
• Choose a project with an existing GitHub Actions pipeline
  • Implement at least 2 of the following optimizations:
station.deploy-stern.challenge.p2: Document the build time before vs. after.
station.deploy-stern.challenge.placehold: Which optimizations did you implement? Build time before vs. after?
station.workflow-nebel.title: The Workflow Nebula
station.workflow-nebel.intro: In the Workflow Nebula you easily lose track: 100 open tabs, full inboxes, and meetings with camera on. Time to examine your digital habits.
station.workflow-nebel.micro.p1: Your digital workday isn't just about code – you work with browsers, emails, chat tools, and video calls. All of this uses resources, even if it feels "light".
station.workflow-nebel.micro.keyfact1: Tab hoarding: 100 open browser tabs can quickly use 1 GB RAM or more. Each tab is its own process with its own memory – even if you haven't looked at it in days.
station.workflow-nebel.micro.list1:
• Chrome is known for high RAM usage – each tab, extension, and service worker needs its own memory
  • Firefox is often more memory-efficient and offers great developer tools
  • Edge (Chromium-based) has a built-in "Sleeping Tabs" mode that automatically suspends inactive tabs
station.workflow-nebel.micro.keyfact2: Email storage: Every attachment, every old email sits on a server – worldwide. An inbox with 5 GB permanently needs server space and electricity for backup and indexing. "Cleaning up" here literally means: saving energy.
station.workflow-nebel.micro.list2:
• A video call with camera needs approx. 1.5–3 Mbit/s upload
  • Camera off reduces bandwidth by up to 96% – to pure audio transmission
  • In a 1-hour meeting with 10 people and cameras, approx. 2–4 GB of data is transferred
  • Not every meeting needs video – often audio or even a chat thread is enough
station.workflow-nebel.micro.keyfact3: Clipboard Manager (e.g. Ditto, CopyQ, macOS Clipboard History) stores your recent copy actions. This saves you from searching again – saving time, tabs, and energy.
station.workflow-nebel.micro.list3:
• Auto-play videos on websites use CPU and bandwidth in the background
  • Push notifications from dozens of apps keep your system constantly active
  • Multiple chat tools simultaneously (Teams, Slack, Discord) multiply RAM usage
station.workflow-nebel.action.p1: Clean up your digital workspace – fewer tabs, less load, more focus.
station.workflow-nebel.action.task1:
• Close all tabs you haven't looked at for more than 1 hour
  • Use bookmarks for pages you want to read "maybe later"
  • Install a tab suspender (e.g. "Auto Tab Discard" for Firefox, "The Great Suspender" for Chrome)
station.workflow-nebel.action.task2:
• Chrome: chrome://system or Task Manager (Shift+Esc)
  • Firefox: about:memory → "Measure"
  • Note the total consumption – does the number surprise you?
station.workflow-nebel.action.task3:
• Delete old emails with large attachments (sort by size)
  • Unsubscribe from 3 newsletters you never read
  • Empty the trash of your email account
station.workflow-nebel.reflection.q: Which of your digital habits would you call "sustainable" – and which not?
station.workflow-nebel.reflection.hint: Sustainable habits are often also more productive habits: Fewer open tabs means more focus. A clean inbox means less search time. Camera off in meetings means less stress. Sustainability and efficiency are not opposites – they reinforce each other.
station.workflow-nebel.challenge.p2: Follow these rules for an entire workday:
station.workflow-nebel.challenge.list:
• Maximum 10 tabs open at once
  • Camera off in all meetings (unless explicitly required)
  • No tab hoarding – anything you don't need in 5 minutes gets closed or bookmarked
  • Only essential tools open – close all apps you're not actively using
  • Use a clipboard manager instead of searching again
station.workflow-nebel.challenge.p3: At the end of the day, write down: What changed? Were you more productive or restricted?
station.workflow-nebel.challenge.placeho: What changed? Were you more productive or restricted? What surprised you?
station.ki-kraftwerk.page_title: The AI Power Plant – Future Metropolis
station.ki-kraftwerk.title: The AI Power Plant
station.ki-kraftwerk.intro: You stand before the pulsing power plant of the metropolis. This is where the energy is generated that keeps artificial intelligence running – but at what cost? Time to look behind the scenes of the AI boom.
station.ki-kraftwerk.micro.p1: Artificial intelligence is everywhere – from ChatGPT to image generation to code assistants. But behind every AI answer lies an enormous energy expenditure that most people completely underestimate.
station.ki-kraftwerk.micro.keyfact1: Training GPT-3 consumed approx. 1,287 MWh of electricity – equivalent to the annual consumption of about 120 Swiss households. Newer models like GPT-4 need many times that.
station.ki-kraftwerk.micro.p2: But it's not just training that's energy-intensive. Every single use – the so-called inference – also costs energy:
station.ki-kraftwerk.micro.list1:
• A single ChatGPT query requires approx. 10 times more energy than a Google search
  • Image generation (e.g. DALL-E, Midjourney) is significantly more energy-intensive than text generation
  • A single AI-generated image uses about as much electricity as fully charging a smartphone
  • Worldwide, AI data centers already consume more electricity than many countries
station.ki-kraftwerk.micro.keyfact2: Estimates suggest AI systems' energy demand could equal the electricity consumption of a country like Sweden by 2027.
station.ki-kraftwerk.micro.p3: But there are also approaches to reduce consumption:
station.ki-kraftwerk.micro.list2:
• Edge AI: Small models running directly on your device – without cloud requests
  • Local AI: Tools like Ollama allow running language models locally
  • More efficient models: Techniques like quantization and pruning drastically reduce energy needs
  • Conscious use: Not every task needs an LLM – sometimes a simple search is enough
station.ki-kraftwerk.action.p1: Check your AI usage behavior: Do you use AI consciously or reflexively? We often reach for ChatGPT when a simpler solution would suffice.
station.ki-kraftwerk.action.task:
• Write down 3 situations where AI is truly useful for you (e.g. code debugging, complex research, translation)
  • Write down 3 situations where AI would be unnecessary (e.g. simple spell-checking, calculator tasks, looking up known facts)
  • Compare: How often do you reflexively reach for AI in everyday life when there would be a more efficient alternative?
station.ki-kraftwerk.action.p2: Tip: Observe yourself over the next few days – and note every time you open an AI tool whether it was really the best choice.
station.ki-kraftwerk.reflection.q: If every AI query costs CO₂ – at what point is the use truly worth it?
station.ki-kraftwerk.reflection.hint: AI is especially worthwhile when it handles tasks that would take disproportionately long manually – or where the quality of the result is clearly better. For simple tasks you could solve yourself in a few seconds, the energy cost of an AI query is hard to justify. The question is not "Can AI do this?" but "Must AI do this?".
station.ki-kraftwerk.challenge.p1: Try going an entire day without AI tools and do everything manually – research, code problems, writing texts.
station.ki-kraftwerk.challenge.p2: Document at the end of the day:
station.ki-kraftwerk.challenge.list:
• Which tasks could you solve without AI without problems?
  • Where did you truly miss AI – and why?
  • What surprised you?
station.ki-kraftwerk.challenge.placehold: What could you solve without AI? Where did you truly miss AI? What surprised you?
station.open-source-platz.page_title: The Open Source Square – Future Metropolis
station.open-source-platz.title: The Open Source Square
station.open-source-platz.intro: Welcome to the bustling square in the heart of the metropolis. Here, knowledge is shared, code is developed openly, and the digital future is built together. Open source is more than a license – it's an attitude.
station.open-source-platz.micro.p1: Open-source software is software whose source code is publicly accessible. Anyone can use, study, modify, and redistribute it. That sounds simple – but has enormous implications for sustainability.
station.open-source-platz.micro.keyfact1: The Linux kernel contains over 30 million lines of code, written by thousands of developers worldwide. This shared work prevents hundreds of companies from developing the same thing separately – saving millions of work hours and resources.
station.open-source-platz.micro.p2: Why Open Source can be more sustainable:
station.open-source-platz.micro.list1:
• No duplicate development: Shared code instead of proprietary isolated solutions – less resource waste
  • Longevity: Open-source projects can be continued by the community even when original developers stop
  • Repairability: Open code can be fixed, adapted, and optimized – even years after release
  • Digital sovereignty: No dependency on a single provider (vendor lock-in)
station.open-source-platz.micro.keyfact2: Vendor Lock-in means becoming dependent on one provider and finding it difficult to switch. This often leads to replacing working systems – not because they're broken, but because the provider wants it that way.
station.open-source-platz.micro.list2:
• MIT License: Very permissive – you can do almost anything with it, including commercially
  • GPL (GNU General Public License): Copyleft – derivative works must also be open source
  • Apache 2.0: Similar to MIT but with explicit patent protection
station.open-source-platz.micro.p4: Open source is no silver bullet: Projects need active maintenance, documentation, and funding. But as a fundamental principle, it promotes a more sustainable digital world.
station.open-source-platz.action.p1: Become part of the open-source community – even with just a small step. You don't have to contribute code right away.
station.open-source-platz.action.task:
• Think of an open-source project you use daily (e.g. VS Code, Firefox, Git, Linux, Node.js, Python)
  • Open its GitHub repository
  • Give the project a Star ⭐ – that shows the maintainers their work is appreciated
  • Read the Contributing Guidelines (CONTRIBUTING.md) – this teaches you how collaboration works in large projects
station.open-source-platz.action.p2: Bonus: Check the Issues – are there any with the label good first issue? Those are entry points for new contributors.
station.open-source-platz.reflection.q: What does open source have to do with sustainability – and what doesn't?
station.open-source-platz.reflection.hin: Open source promotes sustainability through shared resources, longevity, and repairability. But: Open-source software can also be inefficient, waste resources, or be poorly maintained. The license alone doesn't make software sustainable – it depends on the community, the architecture, and conscious development. Sustainability is not a feature but an attitude.
station.open-source-platz.challenge.p1: Make your first open-source contribution! It doesn't have to be code – documentation is extremely valuable too.
station.open-source-platz.challenge.list:
• Bug Report: Found a bug in a tool? Create a well-structured issue on GitHub
  • Improve docs: Find a typo or unclear instruction and create a pull request
  • Translation: Many projects need help with localization – translate a page into German
station.open-source-platz.challenge.p3: Document your contribution with a screenshot or link.
station.open-source-platz.challenge.plac: What contribution did you make? (Link to issue/PR or description)
station.digital-ethics-turm.page_title: The Digital Ethics Tower – Future Metropolis
station.digital-ethics-turm.title: The Digital Ethics Tower
station.digital-ethics-turm.intro: You look up at the tallest building in the metropolis – the Tower of Digital Ethics. From up here you see the big picture: Who is responsible for a sustainable digital world? And what can you as an IT professional do about it?
station.digital-ethics-turm.micro.p1: Digital sustainability is not just a technical question – it has profound ethical dimensions. From planned obsolescence to global injustice to political regulations: As an IT professional, you're right in the middle of it.
station.digital-ethics-turm.micro.keyfac: Planned Obsolescence means products are intentionally designed to break or seem outdated after a certain time. Glued-in batteries, missing spare parts, software updates that slow down old devices – all examples of this.
station.digital-ethics-turm.micro.list1:
• Consumers should have the right to repair their devices themselves
  • Manufacturers should be required to provide spare parts and repair guides
  • The EU already has initial laws: USB-C mandate, Ecodesign Regulation, right to software updates
station.digital-ethics-turm.micro.keyfac: The Rebound Effect: When devices become more efficient, we often simply use them more – and total consumption rises anyway. Example: Faster internet leads to more streaming in higher resolution.
station.digital-ethics-turm.micro.list2:
• About 80% of global e-waste ends up in developing countries – often recycled under health-hazardous conditions
  • The raw materials for our devices (cobalt, lithium, coltan) are frequently mined under inhumane conditions
  • The ecological footprint of digitalization hits hardest those who benefit least from it
station.digital-ethics-turm.micro.keyfac: Sustainable Software Architecture is a growing field: Efficient algorithms, lean data transfer, and modular systems can massively reduce software's resource consumption. The Green Software Foundation develops standards for this.
station.digital-ethics-turm.action.p1: Know your rights: As a consumer, you have more influence than you think – but only if you know what you're entitled to.
station.digital-ethics-turm.action.task:
• Research: What rights do you have in Switzerland regarding device repair?
  • Search for the terms "warranty" and "guarantee" – what's the difference?
  • Find out: Is there a right to repair in Switzerland? What is the EU planning, and how does it affect Switzerland?
  • Check: Does the manufacturer of your laptop or smartphone offer spare parts and repair guides?
station.digital-ethics-turm.action.p2: Tip: Check the websites of iFixit and the FOEN (Federal Office for the Environment) – you'll find useful information there.
station.digital-ethics-turm.reflection.q: Who bears the responsibility for sustainable action in IT – companies, politics, or you yourself?
station.digital-ethics-turm.reflection.h: The answer is: all three – but in different ways. Companies are responsible for product design, supply chains, and transparency. Politics must create frameworks (laws, standards, incentives). And you as a professional and consumer have the power of daily decisions: What do you buy? How long do you use it? How do you develop software? Sustainability doesn't come from a single measure but from the interplay of all actors.
station.digital-ethics-turm.challenge.p1: Create your personal "Digital Sustainability Pledge" – 5 concrete commitments for more sustainable digital work and life.
station.digital-ethics-turm.challenge.p2: Your pledge should include commitments from different areas:
station.digital-ethics-turm.challenge.li:
• Devices: e.g. "I'll use my laptop for at least 5 years"
  • Software: e.g. "I'll pay attention to efficient algorithms in my projects"
  • Data: e.g. "I'll regularly delete unnecessary cloud data"
  • AI: e.g. "I'll only use AI when it truly adds value"
  • Community: e.g. "I'll contribute to open-source projects"
station.digital-ethics-turm.challenge.p3: Write your 5 commitments and share them with a colleague.
station.digital-ethics-turm.challenge.pl: Write your 5 commitments for more sustainable digital work and life here.
station.abschluss-feier.title: 🎉 The Finale Celebration
station.abschluss-feier.intro: You have traversed all four worlds of the learning landscape – the Data Jungle, the Data Ocean, the Code Cosmos, and the Future Metropolis. That's a real achievement! Here is your personal expedition certificate.
station.abschluss-feier.cert_footer: Sustainable Digitalization – Technical Vocational School Zurich
station.abschluss-feier.no_challenges: No challenges submitted yet. Go back to the stations and submit your results!
station.abschluss-feier.reflection_p: What do you take away from this expedition? What will you do differently in your everyday life?

quiz.workflow-nebel.2.q: What saves energy in video calls?
